positive_modes: speaks in declaratives about the past
prefers imperatives and questions, not declaratives
quotes proverbs or texts instead of speaking directly
describes their own actions in present tense as they speak
speaks in conditionals (`if X, then Y`) almost exclusively
usually restates the other character's last line before replying
speaks in numbered or itemized statements
frames replies as a transaction or bargain
speaks only in negations (states what isn't, not what is)
answers questions with warnings or refusals rather than information
attributes their own opinions to absent third parties
interrupts themselves mid-sentence and restarts on a different track
speaks in the future tense, framing utterances as prediction
addresses inanimate objects or absent persons instead of the speaker present
delivers statements as if reading aloud from evidence or testimony
signature_constructions: begins most lines with a preposition
usually names the person they're addressing
uses contractions in most lines where possible
begins most lines with a conjunction
most lines refer to a body part
most lines end in an unanswered implication
rarely uses pronouns of any kind
most lines open with a verb (subject elided)
most lines include a unit of measurement (time, distance, weight, coin)
frames most lines as a comparison (`like X`, `more X than Y`)
speaks usually in sentence fragments, rarely in a complete clause
simile_bans: no `X like Y` similes where Y is domestic or food
no similes comparing the dead or decaying to objects
no extended metaphors longer than one sentence
no similes whose vehicle is a household item
no metaphors built on weather or seasons
no metaphors comparing human action to animal behavior
no similes whose vehicle is a tool or weapon
no metaphors built on light or darkness
no similes whose vehicle is a tradesman's craft
no more than one simile per paragraph
cadence_bans: no staccato comma-list cadences (e.g., `throat, chest, skull`)
no sentences that end on a single-word coda
no parallel-clause balanced sentences (`he did X. she did Y.`)
no consecutive sentences of the same length
no sentences shorter than five words
no scene-ending negation coda (`and X did not Y`)
no `X, then Y, then Z` action sequences
syntactic_bans: no negative-construction sentences (`he did not X`)
no sentences beginning with a participial phrase
no colons used to introduce clauses
imagery_bans: no glowbug, lantern, or torchlight throwing shadows imagery
no birds rising from trees as a reaction to violence
no character described by what their hands are doing
no description of breath as visible vapor
no eyes described as reflective surfaces (mirrors, water, glass)
no silence described as a physical weight
register_bans: no clinical or scientific vocabulary in narration
no archaic register if the scene is modern
no modern slang if the scene is pre-modern
no abstract nouns standing in for sensory description
no philosophical asides from the narrator
no Latinate vocabulary where Anglo-Saxon would do
